History: 'complete atherosclerotic occlusion of the celiac axis with retrograde flow, and severe SMA narrowing'
Labs: 'Alkaline Phosphatase: 124.0 IU/L (ref 35.0-105.0)' — mildly elevated, raising biliary concern
Labs: 'White Blood Cells: 31.5 K/uL' — marked leukocytosis with 'Neutrophils: 93.0%' suggesting acute inflammatory/infectious process

ensemble.reasoning: The patient presents with a new, distinct RUQ pain on top of her known vascular disease, and the CT shows a slightly enlarged gallbladder without the resolution needed to assess wall changes, stones, or ductal dilation. The mildly elevated alkaline phosphatase, marked leukocytosis with neutrophilia, and elevated lactate all raise concern for an acute biliary process (cholecystitis or biliary obstruction) that ultrasound — the gold standard for gallbladder evaluation — is best positioned to confirm or exclude. Ordering a focused gallbladder/liver ultrasound is the logical next step to determine whether the RUQ pain has a surgically or interventionally addressable biliary cause before attributing everything to her known mesenteric vascular disease.

steps.ordered: CT Abdomen (CTA ABD W&W/O C & RECONS)

ensemble.other_hypothesis: Acute mesenteric ischemia (bowel infarction) given known celiac occlusion and severe SMA stenosis; also considering SMA thrombosis/progression, bowel obstruction (given distended stomach and small bowel loops on CXR), and possible acute cholecystitis/acalculous cholecystitis (dilated gallbladder on US)
ensemble.information_gap: Whether the known severe SMA stenosis and celiac occlusion have progressed to acute thrombosis or embolism causing bowel ischemia/infarction, and whether the vascular anatomy is amenable to intervention — the prior CT lacked CTA technique and could not fully characterize the mesenteric vasculature
ensemble.expected_finding: High-grade or complete SMA occlusion/thrombosis, bowel wall thickening or pneumatosis suggesting ischemia, or alternatively patent mesenteric vessels with another source of the acute decompensation (e.g., acalculous cholecystitis, bowel obstruction); the CTA technique will allow detailed arterial phase imaging of the celiac, SMA, and collateral vessels
ensemble.appropriateness_reason: The patient has known celiac occlusion and severe SMA stenosis, a lactate of 2.6 (above normal), leukocytosis of 31.5, elevated anion gap of 24, distended stomach and small bowel on CXR, and new unremitting RUQ pain — a CTA with arterial phase reconstruction is the appropriate next step to evaluate for acute mesenteric vascular compromise that the prior non-CTA CT could not adequately characterize.

The prior CT explicitly noted it lacked CTA technique and could not fully evaluate the mesenteric vasculature. A CTA abdomen with reconstructions is the logical next step to directly visualize the SMA and celiac arterial anatomy, identify any acute thrombosis or embolism, and assess for bowel wall changes consistent with ischemia, while simultaneously evaluating the dilated gallbladder and bowel distension.

verification.actual_finding: The CTA showed a stented, patent SMA (not acutely occluded), persistent celiac occlusion unchanged from prior, and a markedly distended gallbladder with wall thickening, discontinuous mural enhancement, and probable mural necrosis/perforation with adjacent free fluid — consistent with acalculous cholecystitis/gangrenous cholecystitis as the source of acute decompensation.
verification.relation_to_expectation: The physician anticipated either acute SMA thrombosis causing ischemia or an alternative source of decompensation such as acalculous cholecystitis; the CTA confirmed the latter — patent mesenteric vessels with gangrenous cholecystitis as the culprit — exactly as the expected_finding enumerated.
